headlines.title: LAKE INSPECTION SET FOR MONDAY
headlines.subtitle: Trammell Reports to Commission on Clearing of Site.
headlines.fullText: All members of the city commission, Reservoir Engineer Trammell, Land Agent John Davis, City Attorney Me-Cart and Surveyor Brookes Baker will visit the site of Lake Worth for an all-day Inspection Monday. headlines.title: LECTURER CONSIDERS SCHOOL CHILD'S HEALTH
headlines.subtitle: First of Fine Arts Series at First Methodist Church Is Instructive Talk.
headlines.fullText: Dr. J. P. Simonds of the University of Texas, medical department, lectured Friday night at the First Methodist Church on "The Health of the School Child." The discourse, profusely illus-trated with stereopticon views, was one of the most practical presentations of the subject of school welfare ever given In the city. Dr. Simonds has been asked to repeat the lecture for the ben-efit of Fort Worth teachers and par-enta at a later date. The speaker used as his central theme, efficiency. He undertook to show how month and depended cieney of the school child dependea upon his physical well being and He outlined the conditions that affect school health in the wrong way., much outdoor ins promisenous eating and drinking from general drinking cups. diseases, which are much more preva-Jent than is generally known. These Pent la general -umwand other eye troubles, diseases of the car, and nervous diseases. He closed with a plea to adjust the building and curriculum to the child and not try to adjust all kinds of chil-dren to buildings and courses of study- Dr. Simonds returned to Galveston Saturday, where he is a member of the faculty of the medical department. His lecture here opened-me moment of the First Methodist Church,

headlines.title: FORT WORTH BANKERS PICK REPRESENTATIVES
headlines.subtitle: Gage, Harding and Smith to Present City's Claims for Regional Reserve Bank.
headlines.fullText: I. D. Gage, vice president of the First National Bank; Noah Harding, vice president of the Fort Worth Na-tional funk, and Ben O. Smith, prest-dent of the Farmers and Mechanics National Bank, will make Fort Worth's fight for one of the suferameg gional banks when the Texas hearing is conducted at Austin. Feb. 5 and 10. meeting of bankers at the Fort Worth Clearing House Association late day. R. D. Case in Der made for any one city. The hearing will be held by Secre-tary of Agriculture Houston and John Skelton Williams, comptroller of cur-rency. Data supporting Fort Worth's claims for the regional bank were considered have refused to announce the basis of their claims but declare they will make a good showing. The bankers in this meeting were: caut it. C. Kdrington, Traders Na-tional Bank, president; W. E. Connell, First National Bank; Maj. K. M. Van Zandt. Fort Worth National Bank; Ben H. Martin, Farmers and Mechan-les National Bank; G. H. Colvin, Amer-lean National Bank: " mm Western National Bank; John Sparks, Stock Yards National Bank; J. E. Willis, Continental Bank and The Company: State Bank: Worm wood Clarence Parker, North Texas State Bank: I. B. Comer, Fort Worth State Bank.

headlines.title: MAKERS OF PORCELAIN STIRRED BY U. S. SUIT
headlines.subtitle: Government Seeks to Recover $1,220,000 From Haviland Company on
headlines.fullText: LIMOGES, France - 31-Great Interest has been aroused among por-celain manufacturers by the sult that against Theodore Haviland & Co., to recover 11,220,000 alleged to be due on imports undervalued by fraudulent in-voicen. Most of the manufateurers here ex-port their wares to the United States under the same conditions as did the Haviland company. The latter, al-though an American house, was Incor-porated In France and has a factory in Limoges.

headlines.title: ELECTRICIANS HONOR MONTGOMERY, LEAVING
headlines.subtitle: Short Talks Made by Number of Contractors at Banquet-Paddleson Toastmaster.
headlines.fullText: Electrical contractors of Fort Worth gave Robert W. Montgomery, for more than two years new-business manager for the Fort Worth Power and Light Company, a farewell banquet Friday night at the Westbrook Montgomery naves tonight for Louisville, where he has accepted a similar, position. C. L Paddleson, president of the Electrical toastmaster. Mesle man sunnites bar Miss Hazel Winans Selby, cabaret singer at the Westbrock. Short talks were made by A. J. An-derson, E. A. Shotta, M. F. Wertham. f . sous - Wortham Dickey, Paul Stieren, H. R. Ashley, P. L. Pritchett, George Bound and D. J. Pritchett. Others present were Herbert Vous, Harry W. Thompson. B. R. Blair and D. Otto.

headlines.title: WILL EXAMINE 20 'MOVIE' OPERATORS
headlines.subtitle: Three Tested for License Under New Ordinance and Past Questioned
headlines.fullText: Twenty "movie" machine operators will be examined next Tuesday at city hall for permits under the new motion picture show ordinance. The examinations will be both oral and written. They will be conducted by City Electrician Crabtree, City Fire Marshal Lennon and City Building In-spector Allen. Three were tested Pri-a afternoon and passed. The examination consists of quen-tions regarding the machines used in showing reelt. They are required on The ground that the Ignition of fim the booth is so constructed that fire could not spread into the auditorium of the theater.

headlines.title: ABUNDANT NOTICE IS GIVEN OBSCURED AD
headlines.subtitle: Error in Figure Keeps Stripling's Busy Answering Phone Saturday.
headlines.fullText: Everybody who advertises knows that Star-Telegram ads are reid closely. but just how closely was unter otter shown than In The cure ttsement us - paper. Down near the bottom of the ad, in small type, was this line, "30e bleached. 10-4 wide sheeting for 10c." Of course it was wrong, but the A til Stripling the calls store became had not so numerous noticed It that un- an inquiry was started and the error discovered. The price should have been 25 cents.
